posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked: I personally much preferred Pierre Damoy …

I think at the top, Chambertin and LSJ, they can’t be beat, but I think the issue is with the rest of the portfolio and pricing. I would have to agree with the OP when discussing the full range not just 2 wines.

We had the 14’ Ruchottes before Christmas and while delicious not worth the $300+ wholesale…

There’s often serious disconnect between how the critics see the top producers (grouped pretty closely together) and how many consumers see the top producers (massive spreads in quality and price). With all respect to the latter (consumers), there tends to be a lot of groupthink among the latter that becomes self-reinforcing that can make two wines with minimal qualitative differences become crazily sought after on the one hand and kind of dismissed on the other - because no one wants even slightly second best and everyone wants to believe in the magic of owning “the best.” Though it actually makes buying wines like Trapet the smart move more often than not.

The self-reinforcing nature of one wine means it catches fire in a major way, both in price and perception. Consumers fall all over themselves to pile on buying, price goes up, and then so do the justifications for paying that top price. No one is immune to this effect.

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked:
I have been fortunate to have had tasted Rousseau many many times. And I started buying Rousseau long time ago and thus Rousseau constitutes approx 20% of my Red Burgundies.

They are Wonderful wines. I love the Chambertin though it takes significantly more time to show its best compared to Clos de Beze. CSJ and Ruchottes are excellent too and I also love the Clos de la Roche. Latter is a very feminine expression compared with the likes of Dujac etc but very seductive indeed. My experience with Mazis and Charmes is a it limited (99, 01, 02, 05, 06, 07, 08). Charmes are charming, Rousseau like but do not have the depth or layers of complexity. Mazis can be a bit obdurate and take many years to unflur. The recent vintages post 2005 are much better for the ‘lesser Rousseau wines’. I have had the Cazetiers only at ‘release’ tasting but not with age on them. '05 and 08 Cazetiers were really super (when tasted in full horizontals compared to all their costlier stablemates).

I stopped buying Rousseau after 2012 as I lost my allocation and the wines became super expensive.

Are they Top Tier? YES. They may not be overtly big and powerful as the Mortet or Trapet but their elegance, grace and poise is undeniable. They also are very open and have a long drinking window.

Are they worth the money? I have difficulty buying them at current prices as I always think back and say OMG only few years ago they costed so much less.

posts.name: Martin Buchanan
posts.cooked:
The point Jim is making here is a tough one to make and i praise him for it.

I think middle-aged Rousseau wines are delicious and surprisingly accessible for a top producer. For me, the question is with 25-40 years age how do they compare to the top handful of producers? This is not a pejorative question but an open one.

Rousseau 99s and recently the 01s in particular are super impressive but i have had only a couple of wines from the 80s and back that have really blown me away. Obviously the sample is relatively small.

With proper age, I am consistently more excited by Roumier and DRC, especially Roumier (although there is a sense of chalk and cheese twice here). Yet tasting middle-aged wines from DRC and to some extent Roumier is tough. With the exception of 98, for most of the late 90s - mid 2000 vintages, the Rousseau 3 are more enjoyable now. i would not however bank on that remaining the case.

On a slightly less fancy level, I would make a case for the potential quality of the Bruno Clair wines, as illuminated in wines of Clair Dau. I would expect Rousseau to taste much more grand and exciting than Bruno Clair from a vintage like 99 but i wouldn’t bank on this remaining the case in 10-15 years time. I think oak has a part to play here.

Regarding Trapet etc. It’s an interesting call by Bettane et al. I would agree that many Dominique Laurent wines have come good. By no means all of them but 00 for sure and i would make a case for 01 too and remarkably 94. I think on the basis of past insight these guys deserve our respect and consideration. Mortet is particularly tricky to call because Arnaud is building on Denis’ work but the change is becoming more obvious vintage on vintage. I was once blinded on Fourrier CSJ 1999, Rousseau CSJ 1999 and Mortet Lavaux 1999. I said that the Mortet was the Rousseau; the Fourrier was the Fourrier and the Rousseau was the Mortet because the oak on the Rousseau stuck out and the Mortet was f*cking awesome. I would have expected the Mortet to be overoakedbut heh…

Is the question here maybe deliciousness versus authenticity? Of course, obviously these overlap.

If we extend this to the Cote de Beaune and take in Lafarge and Pousse D’Or this gets even more interesting. Can we define tradition in Burgundy in terms of sentiment and intention rather than method?

Fun topic.

Cheers,
Martin

ITB UK - so probably a bit of fusty f*cker

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
We are at a point where the premium for critic favorites are extreme. Producers like Rousseau, Mugnier and Roumier, for example, make great wines but there are a number of other producers who, IMHO, also make great wines from the same appellations that sell for a fraction of the price.

For example, Rousseau Chambertins go for about $1500-2000. Those from Rossignol-Trapet go for $200-300.

Roumier les Cras sells for $300-500 or so. Barthod for $150-200.


Mugnier Fuees sells for $250-300. Jadot for a bit over $100.

We could go on and on.

Great, thoughtful first post here, Martin (praise for me aside ). I hope you’ll stay and contribute more.

Part of the difficulty is that these wines are made in tiny qualities, and few if any of us get to try (or, frankly, have tried) them extensively as they move from youth to middle age to full maturity. Your point about the 99 LSJs is one that I have raised on occasion - certain producers were deemed as “overoaked” during that era and vintage. Mortet being one, and Perrot-Minot certainly being another. I have not been able to track Mortet much past 05, but I have been able to taste the evolution of Perrot-Minot from the mid-90s until now, and there has been a significant evolution in style from (by Christophe’s own admission) from genuinely over-oaked and extracted to much more gentle handling now (I would say 04 forward, with 06 being a more certain breaking point). However, most consumer-based criticism of P-M discusses the style of a different and earlier era.

I think it’s worth approaching great wines like these with less certainty about any hierarchy. It has served me well in exploring.

I do think deliciousness and authenticity overlap. I happen to think some of the misguided professional criticism of DRC stemmed from (pun somewhat intended) being hard to approach young. Rousseau is, in my admittedly limited experience, been among the most approachable early of the “great” producers.

What makes “tradition” in Burgundy seems like a minefield (h/t RMP) - though a fun one if no one takes differences of opinions personally.

I can’t recall exactly when Jean-Louis took over, but quality jumped in a major way in the late 90s/early 00s and kept improving. Before, there were great wines from time-to-time (I had a killer 59 Clos Prieur from Trapet at 45 years), but no consistency, which was true of many addresses in the era you mention. A lot of top estates became beloved by becoming consistently great first - and providing consistent quality inspired younger generations elsewhere to follow in their footsteps.

It’s easy to forget that we’re living in a golden age for the region. The wines were all over the map for most of our lifetimes. Consistent quality across producers and appellations is, IMHO, a much more recent development than it might seem.

posts.name: Martin Buchanan
posts.cooked:
Thanks Jim. I have been lurking too long.

I think Larry raises a good point about the understanding we extend to established producers. I think it is right to do so because wines are often opened too young / wrong temp / with the wrong food etc. however it is dangerous.

The track record can also offer reassurance about what kind of experience we can expect. Rousseau delivers a lot of pleasure reliably to a wide audience encompassing many non burg enthusiasts. I note that a number of the best loved producers have a distinct individual style: Rousseau, Coche, Pierre Morey era Leflaive, Raveneau. I don’t question the quality of any of these but I wonder if familiarity makes them even more enjoyable.

Where things have changed recently i.e. Perrot-Minot and Mortet I would be reluctant to try and nail down the style or quality level. I am excited by both, especially Perrot-Minot but i think they are both experimenting and developing.

I think the real value (if one can still say that about Burgundy) lies in producers who have the track record but lie just off the radar. For me, Bruno Clair is the top one here. We can sum up the whole thread in 1 mathematical function I came up years ago for fun that links quality and price:

Take wine1: its score is S1 and its Price is P1
Take wine2: its score is S2 and its Price is P2

If S2 > S1, then P2 = exp(S2/S1) * P1

So a difference of 1 point in score leads to ratio of 2.7 in price. To that, you still need multiply by the hype factor of the vintage where resellers increase their margins even more. For a vintage like 2015, the hype factor is going to be around 2.5.
So the real formula is
P2 = exp(S2/S1) * P1 * HypeFactor

If other words, it means that price goes up exponentially as score increases (by increment). It is wine critics vs group thinking in numbers, amplified by the vintage hype

Let’s see with 2015
Chambertin Trapet is at 97 according to Burghound, Rousseau is at 98
Theoretical price difference should be exp(98/97) = 2.75. But then hype factor is high for 2015 so it is going to be a ratio of 2.75*2.5 = 6.9

On WS, Trapet 2015 is at $400.
There is no Rousseau available for 2015, I would expect Rousseau to be priced at $2760.

Based upon my limited experience, Rousseau is not overrated. Instead it is often underrated. The Chambertin and CdB are some of the few grand crus that have – in my limited experience – been astoundingly delicious at every stage of their development – young, somewhat young, medium, somewhat older medium, youngish-but-aged, aged, and questionable due to age. Also, while my Rousseau experience is largely limited to Chambertin/Beze/ClosSt.Jaques, every Rousseau I’ve had has been pretty damn amazingly delicious.

Here’s another POV re: why Rousseau is “underrated”, not overrated. (Of course, any “rated” begs the question of what one means…top wines are top; accross the stable, etc.?)

I visited Rousseau reguarly from 1990-2007…and bought wines from them almost every year in between. (A big hunk of my cellar). But, I don’t look at the quality of a domaine as being determined by the quality of its top wines only, but by its whole stable. And, there isn’t a better stable in all of Burgundy…except for DRC, I guess. And, I always wanted and was impressed more with the “lesser grands crus” there: Ruchottes, Clos de la Roche and particularly, Mazy-Chambertin which, IMO, are wonderful wines year after year, and as Greg pointed out, show well at every stage [after ten years out…] The values on them are extraordinary, IMO. They are not as flashy as their big 3 mainly because of the limited/no use of NFO on their elevages. Treat Mazy like Charles Rousseau [over]treated the CSJ and over-esteemed it vis a vis other grand crus there…and their Mazy would be legendary. Ditto for their Ruchottes, which does get some. (And their Clos de la Roche has some potential, too.)

Take the estate as a whole…it’s as “good” and as any estate making red wine in Burgundy (except DRC, which I’ve never bought or really experienced). But, nobody focuses on the real “hidden” treasures there…when evaluating it. Only the big 3. Put them all together and they spell…“magic”. (And, for me, they made the best '95s I’ve tasted.)

Really good winemaking…although filtered pretty well, and, therefore, polished (for a particular reason)…and GREAT holdings…and plenty of resources to do it right. An invulnerable combo, IMO.

The big 3 are the tip of the iceberg, albeit a golden tip. And…the two Chambertins are , IMO, as good as any red grand cru I’ve ever experienced.

Underrated, from here…and so much potential…if they shared some NFO on the “lesser” wines…

Now, the cost is a separate thing…

Stuart - really interesting post. Do you have a sense of why Rousseau has such a dramatic difference in oak treatment for the big three versus some of the other wines? Lots of good vintners feel like the grand crus that Rousseau is producing can handle more new oak. Seems to me that if you prefer less oak, maybe the non-big three are the way to go.

There were two factors when Charles Rousseau was in charge:

  1. His own preference for CSJ , which was his first purchase after his father died in a car accident in the '50s. He, IMO, overvalued it, in terms of how much oak it could use and benefit from, treating it like the two Chambertins, which clearly do benefit.

  2. Estates in Burgundy have to buy their barrels long before the harvest, guessing on the yields at flowering or earlier. Rousseau often had some new barrels left over, depending on the vintage. So, Charles would “give” some to Ruchottes first (he engineered the purchase of that for himself, Dr. Georges Mugneret and Bonnefond, keeping the “clos”). Only if there was really lots of extra new barrels would anything else see any…when Charles was in charge. So, it was vintage by vintage what the “overflow” of NFO was.

The Mazy has always been my “sweet spot”/qpr champ there. With some NFO, it would outshine all but the Chambertins, IMO.
CSJ is good…but can’t really benefit from 100% NFO.

It will be interesting to see what Eric’s daughter does…I don’t think Eric was even consulted when charles was in charge…Charles was supposedly a control freak…in the winery. In all my visits, from 1990-2007, I never met Eric…he was behind the scenes for some reason. Charles did allow his daughter, Corinne, to show people around in the mid-90s for a decade or so…but was always hovering. (He seemed to me to be a very good guy and people in Burgundy thought the sam; whether Eric did, too, I don’t know. )

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Rousseau have quite extensive holdings, and there is some variation in vine genetics, vine age and farming across them. Since the addition of the holdings of the Château de Gevrey-Chambertin (in Gevrey villages, LSJ and Charmes-Chambertin), the picture has become even more complicated. My understanding is that the domaine’s vines in Charmes-Chambertin, for example, are young and tend to crop at the appellation maximum. FWIW, to my palate, the Cazetiers is the most “under-rated” wine in the cellar. I’m not convinced by the Charmes or the Clos de la Roche, though between the mid-2000s and 2012 or so they did seem to be improving. To really measure their quality, it’s not enough to say that they’re very nice wines: one must compare them with the benchmarks in their respective appellations. So while Rousseau Charmes may be a very tasty bottle of Pinot Noir, it would be difficult to claim it’s on a par with, e.g. Denis Bachelet’s (and one could think of a handful of others too) in terms of depth, dimension, character and concentration.

Whether as critics or consumers, it’s important to acknowledge manifest greatness without descending into blind veneration. Whether it’s Rousseau, or some of the others cited here as Domaines that can do no wrong (DRC and Liger-Belair have both been nominated), no domaine is immune to ups and downs, traumatic generational transitions, or even just unhappy accidents: for example, as is well known, most of Rousseau’s 1979s and some of their 1978s had to be sent to the distillery because of microbiological spoilage. Now another generational transition is underway chez Rousseau, and we should all be following the wines with interest.

Louis-Michel Liger-Belair likes to say that a grand cru is ‘10% wine, 90% imagination’. That’s as true of Burgundy’s great domaines as it is of its greatest vineyards.

All that said by way of a qualifying preamble, the Rousseau Chambertin is clearly some way ahead of Trapet’s, and it is indeed perplexing that anyone would suggest otherwise.

As for the lesser ones I noticed a major step up in quality from 2012. Specially the Charmes took a big step up the ladder. Regarding the Chambertin, the 2010 is the vintage to buy. It is towering over everything else I have tasted including several vintages of Romanée Conti. The CSJ 2014 is also something very special. I was surpriced and blown away by this wine from this overlooked vintage.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked: To really measure their quality, it’s not enough to say that they’re very nice wines: one must compare them with the benchmarks in their respective appellations. So while Rousseau Charmes may be a very tasty bottle of Pinot Noir, it would be difficult to claim it’s on a par with, e.g. Denis Bachelet’s (and one could think of a handful of others too) in terms of depth, dimension, character and concentration.

I’m not sure that is the best example, as most of Rousseau’s charmes is mazoyeres and Bachelet’s sections are, as I understand it, charmes proper that sits across from Chambertin in as privileged a position as one could ask for. I get that one can label mazoyeres as charmes, but it doesn’t seem that everything labelled charmes is really an apples to apples comparison. Roumier labels his charmes as mazoyeres of course, but doesn’t seem to think of it as a grand cru terroir (at least according to the sources that I’ve read).

One might even go so far as to draw the opposite conclusion and determine that given the relative sources of what they are producing, Rousseau makes a pretty impressive bottle of charmes.

While it’s true that the terroirs can be quite different (though parts of Mazoyeres are quite like parts of Charmes), I’m not sure they’re so very far away for the comparison to be entirely otiose at this level; and I suspect that Bachelet’s old vines, cropped at Bachelet’s yields, would nevertheless make a wine very different from Rousseau’s were they growing in Rousseau’s parcel. But, accepting your point for the sake of argument, it would also be interesting to compare Rousseau’s Charmes with Roumier’s. After that, we could compare the Clos de la Roche with Hubert Lignier’s and Dujac’s.

This is not intended to disparage Rousseau, a domaine I love as much as anyone (there are few bottles in my cellar I wouldn’t change for another bottle of even their 1964 village), but just to lobby for a touch more realism when it comes to the quality of Rousseau’s ‘lesser’ grand crus these days, which now all carry very hefty price tags and can legitimately be held to the highest standards.

Rousseau’s prices at Released :

Here in Quebec Canada the government regulates the released prices for all wines.

I could easily buy any Rousseau’s wine in the 1990s…and until around vintage 2002.

Now…if I would like to buy any Rousseau’s wines ( at the released prices dictated by the Government ) I need to register my name in any order form and join the lottery system organized by the Government here…

Rousseau’s prices at the Secondary market:

Auction market only started in USA in 1994.

Meadows used to post in Parker"s B in the late 1990s under his alias BURG HOUND.

In the year 2000 he started Burghound. He stated to report and post results of many his comparative tasting of many mini horizontal of CdB and Chambertin from many prodcuers. Rousseau always ends up as his favorite.

Hence my comments and hence the raise of Rousseau’s big 2 in the Auction ( or secondary ) market which seldom exits in Canada.

Wish I keep a record of Rousseau’s big 2 in the Auction market in the USA between 1995 to 1999. Then maybe we will have a better idea…

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
Fred C:
A Songeur:

So how do you rate Rousseau Ruchottes versus Mugneret Gibourg Ruchottes?
Irrespective of price…

For me it’s hard to take price out of the equation when the Mugneret Gibourg version is now more than the Rousseau by $100 or more!

For my palate, I put Rousseau and MG at the top. It is all about supply and deman as you know.

2014 Gevrey served Blind




  • 2014 Domaine Georges Mugneret/Mugneret-Gibourg Ruchottes-Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Ruchottes-Chambertin Grand Cru
    Absolutely exquisite nose displaying pure black fruit with a hint of red, crushed blackberry, crushed black cherry, raspberry, rose, sap, espresso bean, sesame, incense and vosne spice. Very pure and energetic palate, finely layered perfectly ripe blackberry, bright acidity, excellent mineral presence and am incredibly long pure black fruit and espresso bean driven finish. It is beguilingly rich and viscous yet also subtle and weightless. I drank a small pour of the 14 Vosne the night before and the overall expression is quite similar, although naturally more complex and layered. The purity in fruit and impeccably balanced palate lead me to MG Ruchottes. (97 pts.)
  • 2014 Domaine Fourrier Griotte-Chambertin Vieille Vigne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Griotte-Chambertin Grand Cru
    Big scale and slightly masculine nose displaying very ripe red fruit with a hint of black, strawberry and rhubarb tart, cherry pie, a hint of blackberry, a hint of spearmint that quickly dissipates, sap, sweet spices and earth. Excellent concentration, layers upon layers of rich concentrated red fruit, good acidity and earthy mineral, very fine tannins and a long concentrated red fruit and sap driven finish. The overall expression is masculine initially but softens up after a couple of hours. According to Ken, decanted for three hours. I am not a fan of decanting young red Burgundy as it tightens the nose, losing the opulent primary fruit and perfume/floral expression. The earlier CT notes indicate a lot of finesse. This particular bottle is masculine and dense, especially compare to the Mugneret-GIbourg and Rousseau Ruchottes. This is a big scale wine that will require two more decades to reach full maturity. My guess is correct. (94 pts.)
  • 2014 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Ruchottes-Chambertin Clos des Ruchottes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Ruchottes-Chambertin Grand Cru
    Beautiful harmonious nose displaying crushed blackberry, a hint of raspberry, caramel, sap, espresso bean and limestone. Already showing beautiful harmonious palate, silky and fluid, finely layered fresh blackberry and espresso bean driven palate impression, perfect amount of acidity and mineral, incredibly fine tannins and a seamless pure black and red fruit driven finish with a hint of sap and espresso bean at the end. My guess is the Fourrier CSJ but the finesse makes me to quickly correct myself as the Rousseau. With air, the nose and palate become sensual and perfectly harmonious. (96 pts.)
  • 2014 Domaine Fourrier Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Clos St. Jacques Vieille Vigne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru
    Similar nose as the Rousseau initially but a bit more meatier and riper. Expressive nose displaying crushed blackberry, fresh cranberry sauce, a hint of strawberry and rhubarb tart, strong sap and earth. Excellent concentration, packed perfectly ripe blackberry, dense, bright acidity, strong earthy mineral, very fine tannins and a long crushed blackberry driven finish. With two hours of air, the fruit gets brighter, more precise and redder. This will also take two more decades to reach the peak. Really nice showing. (95 pts.)

I too have not become a socialist but the obscene amounts of money people are paying for these bottles is making me lean a little more to the left. These people may claim that they are entitled to do whatever they want with their money but the problem is that most of them are either isolated from or do not care about the enormous and increasing discordance in wealth. I am exposed to this in my involvement with patients of lower economic classes, and it makes me feel guilty to indulge more than say $100 on a bottle.

As per this study, the brain pleasure responses are higher when, at least these subjects, thought that the same wine was more expensive.

http://www.pnas.org/content/105/3/1050.long

As per my post #64, in response to your post, I agree that there are different cultural classes within the wine world. I know you are a European and a member of the GJE, and I think that puts us in a different culture from what we are seeing in this topic were people a more concerned about the price of the wine and how one wine compares to another . As I said in post 64, such aspects of wine almost never come up when I am drinking with people such as Rousseau.

Perhaps the problem is that these other cultures know nothing about wine other than what is on the label and how much it cost. Perhaps if they actually knew the wine makers, they would appreciate the incredible character and passion that most of them demonstrate through their actions and lives. I know four generations of one wine making family in Pomerol, and three generations of a family in St. Emilion, and learning how they approach life certainly affects my wine appreciation. The current director of the St. Emilion winery told me that the most important thing about a wine is the “quality of the visit”. I doubt few people here could understand what that means.

Anyway looking forward to seeing you at the WWS in November, where we will obviously not be talking about wine in a competitive sport manner.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
I think we have established that Rousseau’s top 3 are not overrated (and others can be pretty special but not necessarily the best expressions of those vineyards).

Now, as to whether they are overpriced, it depends if we are talking about normal retail pricing or “gouge” pricing. Over the years I have been able to buy fairly consistently at normal retail, meaning normal retail markups over wholesale through regular distribution channels. These prices are higher than those fortunate subscribers who pick up their allocations at the domaine each year, but significantly less than grey market or auction pricing or the 500% markups that retailers around the country sell them for. Of course even these retail prices have climbed over the years from the $150-250 Chambertins of the late 90’s and early 2000’s.

So, just curious. If you could buy your Rousseau Chambertins to drink (not to resell) at $600 per bottle, how many of you would buy the bottle or few you were offered?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Interesting retreading this thread. I still agree with those who say that Rousseau is a top producer period. But at the current prices, I cannot bring myself to buy them. So I will buy Trapet and Rossignol Trapet, and be almost completely happy. Almost.

After to Rousseau is Trapet your next fav for Chambertin?

Yes. I think the wines are not far from what I remember Rousseau used to be, but the last one I tasted young (apart from 2 oz tasting samples) was 2002.

To answer another question, yes I would be buying the two Rousseau Chambertins at $600 and CSJ at $400. This has never been about quality, it has always been about a price that works. That price is a lot higher for other people than myself.

To be honest, I sold too early. When prices reached $1200 for Rousseau, they were gone, on the basis that except for tastings, I would never spend that much on my own.

Perhaps a function of some really top reviews over the past several vintages.

posts.name: Emmanuel Menger
posts.cooked:
It seems that the Trapet Chambertin’s are now hitting that $500-600 range in the more “sought-after” vintages…slightly less on occasion at an auction, though not sure what their “normal retail” pricing is vs. what I see on the internet. Perhaps a function of some really top reviews over the past several vintages.

This is merely a change in pricing policy that first occured with the 2016 vintage. 2015 benefited from top scores but was still a bargain when compared to other chambertins of the same breed (~240/250€). Price has then dramatically skyrocketed at the domaine with new vintages being offered at around 350-400. I really doubt this policy meets a great success at least in Europe. No upside at auction, 50pct price increase impulsed overnight are not good ingredients to draw buyers’ attention.

posts.name: Emmanuel Menger
posts.cooked:
Doubtful…any true wine-lovers buy wines to re-sell. Most likely their taste changed and circumstances changed…

chambertin rousseau is sold around €450 by the domaine. If you buy it to a very nice wine merchant in France you may get it at around 700-800, provided you purchase a huge number of other wines of lesser pedigree. At auction it seems to me that prices have reached a kind of plateau as I dont see many vintages sold above 1700/1800 (excl. buyers premium) with a majority of sales deeply anchored in the 1300-1500 zone. Hence from a pricing perspective the recent vintages of the domaine are not as hot as they were 3 years ago. Buyers are more interested in the ultra rare stuff such as d´auvenay and leroy

posts.name: Josh Najjar
posts.cooked:
We are at a point where the premium for critic favorites are extreme. Producers like Rousseau, Mugnier and Roumier, for example, make great wines but there are a number of other producers who, IMHO, also make great wines from the same appellations that sell for a fraction of the price.

For example, Rousseau Chambertins go for about $1500-2000. Those from Rossignol-Trapet go for $200-300.

Roumier les Cras sells for $300-500 or so. Barthod for $150-200.


Mugnier Fuees sells for $250-300. Jadot for a bit over $100.

We could go on and on.

Howard, in my opinion, the Mugnier - Fuees, is one of the top premier crus in all of Burgundy.
I own some Jadot - Fuees, but have yet to sample it. Are you saying the Jadot Fuees is in the same league at as the Mugnier ? I would be pleasantly surprised if you are correct !

posts.name: emile bond
posts.cooked:
Rousseau Chambertin & Beze 2016 were $680/btl, give or take, Wholesale depending the Distributor GP. $910/btl retail would be 25% GP for the retailer based on $680/btl Wholesale price. Most prices I saw were $1500+/btl. $629 USD for Chambertin 2016 is an absolute steal from a North American perspective. It also conveys how much less margin was being taken between the cellar door and cash register when buying in Canada compared to buying in US.

At 600$, I would buy 1 bottle Chambertin and 1 bottle Beze… Never been offered though…tough luck…

Last I saw here in Quebec (April of last year) were the 2017 offered through a lottery (as are most highly sought after bottles). I don’t have the exact number of bottles offered anymore for the Chambertin or the Clos de Bèze but it was probably around 10 to 20 bottles for the entire province of Quebec (population of 8.5 million… obviously not 8.5 million potential Rousseau Chambertin customers). The pricing was 1,009.00 CAD (approx. 724 USD back then). The 2016 were at 842.00 CAD (629 USD in April 2019). Nice little bump in one year…! How does that compare with the US pricing?

I can also say that at the price, there are a lot of bidders over here. I never got my hands on one.

I very much like Claude dugat wines that I buy ex estate and from a few wine merchants but I have never found their charmes as good as the griotte which remains in my view THE outstanding wine of the estate. Bachelet and roty which i also buy directly ex estates are truly special. I wont repeat what professionnals say about Roty (you should read the excellent article from S. Tanzer on Roty) except that these wines deserve a considerable snooze in the cellar to give their best. I would not touch a bachelet and a roty charmes before their 15th anniversary at least, whereas I could easily pop up a Charmes Rousseau of less than 10y. I dont believe the latter deserves a long time in the cellar as the level of extra complexity will in my view never be massive.
With respect to clos de la roche rousseau: I dont buy it too much as I had mixed experiences in recent past, most recently a 2011 which was weak and greeny.

I’ve had a bunch of older Claude Dugat and have found the style massively extracted and very much not my thing (I appreciate this has changed in recent vintages). At Paulee last year someone offered to pour me some before saying “how would you like a very different expression of pinot noir” . We then proceded to have a great conversation about Michel Grisard
I haven’t read any articles on Roty, but I’ve never had one younger than 2008 (and most older), and they drink rustic to me and also with a consistent herbal streak in all the wines that I don’t especially like. Based on old Roty wines, I’m not a huge fan.
I’ve never had Bachelet’s Charmes so can’t comment, but I’ve never been thrilled by his village or Corbeaux, and the current pricing on the Cobreaux is already quite a bit beyond my willingness to pay for that wine. They’re good wines, but they’re not good enough that I’m prepared to seek out the Charmes at US pricing.

I don’t consider a green 2011 Rousseau a meaningful data point - there are lots of green wines in 2011 from great producers. A 2011 Suchots from Hudelot Noellat I had this past summer was so green it was undrinkable - it did not stop me buying his 15s, 16s or 17s. The 2012 and 2013 Rousseau Clos de la Roches I’ve had recently have been fantastic.

posts.name: emile bond
posts.cooked:
2/3 of Rousseau Charmes is Mazoyeres vines.
Roty and Bachelet are 100% Charmes proper vines, if memory serves.
Some may contend herein explains at least part of the difference between the wines.


To be honest, I have less experience with Rousseau charmes than I have with its peers, namely claude dugat, bachelet, roty. I always had the impression that charmes rousseau was first a rousseau though obviously not on par with the 3 big boys. It does not possess the sheer intensity of the big guns but remains a delicious drink for people who value drinkability and pleasure first and foremost. To the extent you dont focus too much on complexity of aromas, you will like it very much and certainly enjoy a great moment.

I know this is often applied to Rousseau’s Clos de la Roche, but given how good that has been the past 10 years or so, I’m not sure I overly care. It’s interesting that you view Bachelet and Roty as the “big guns” of Charmes - I’ve yet to have a breakthrough experience with either producer. (I have generally avoided older Claude Dugat due to the style, though I appreciate the style has changed significantly in recent years - but with a premium.)

I very much like Claude dugat wines that I buy ex estate and from a few wine merchants but I have never found their charmes as good as the griotte which remains in my view THE outstanding wine of the estate. Bachelet and roty which i also buy directly ex estates are truly special. I wont repeat what professionnals say about Roty (you should read the excellent article from S. Tanzer on Roty) except that these wines deserve a considerable snooze in the cellar to give their best. I would not touch a bachelet and a roty charmes before their 15th anniversary at least, whereas I could easily pop up a Charmes Rousseau of less than 10y. I dont believe the latter deserves a long time in the cellar as the level of extra complexity will in my view never be massive.
With respect to clos de la roche rousseau: I dont buy it too much as I had mixed experiences in recent past, most recently a 2011 which was weak and greeny.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
“I mean market price. Most people don’t have access to standard retail markup.”

Agree. “Retail” used to mean the relative standard markups on wholesale pricing set by the importer or distributer, I suppose. Now it means what the market will bear via the grey market and per online retailers. I live in WA state, where retailers cannot import wines or buy grey market, and must buy from a state licensed distributor, so this isn’t the open range I am fortunate enough to be a close friend of a retailer who has generously sold me an allocation of Rousseau over the past 40 years at his normal retail markup over his wholesale price. (And, BTW, the allocations by the time they hit WA state are not huge.) He could have made more money by selling at auction or marking the wines up ridiculously, or he could have just taken them home for his own enjoyment. But he did not…he honored our long term relationship. As the auction and grey market prices have risen, the idea of “retail pricing” has become “internet pricing”, and I am sure that for scarce items some brick and mortar retailers have significantly raised their pricing to even their established customers after seeing what the wines are selling for via the internet.

Well, it is what it is, but when I think of "retail’ pricing, I think of what I paid for the last vintage, the 2016 Rousseau Chambertin (in our corner of the country, the 2017’s have not yet appeared), and it was less than $700 (I got one bottle). Got some more of the other crus. I am sure that more than a few of the members of WB have these long term relationships with their local retailers, and have been buying these NOW scarce wines year in and year out over countless vintages, and they also see these wines at more “normal” prices, what I would call “retail” pricing, meaning standard markup over wholesale, and not “what the market will bear” pricing.

But, on the other hand, there are so many Burgundy producers we don’t even see distributed in WA, and those we see are in small allocations with the upper level wines sometimes absent, and the pricing in general is higher through normal channels compared to internet pricing for the non “highly sought after” wines, as there is no great amount of competitive pricing…so whatever.

I am sorry to go on so, and perhaps I am just old. But Rousseau Chambertin on release at $2500 is not normal pricing, and should not be viewed as such. It may be all there is available for most, but it is speculative and not retail. Of course, I cannot buy Raveneau Clos at less than $600 via the internet, and I have no local allocation, so I no longer buy it, and that sucks.

posts.name: emile bond
posts.cooked:
Clos des Ruchottes owned by Rousseau is the more elegant, mineral, fine-boned wine from the vineyard. It’s been seemingly an under appreciated wine chez Rousseau until the last decade or so. It’s a gorgeous wine, and better farming along with climate change may have added more flesh to the wine this century. Since the Clos is arguably the coolest section of the vineyard, wine from the Clos may not have been as obviously generous in another era. The Clos is at the top of the vineyard with the steepest aspect, shallower soil, at the highest elevation of vineyard, and receives shade from trees at the top of the vineyard earlier in the afternoon than lower down in the vineyard. M-G plot is located in the Ruchottes du Bas section of the vineyard, which is the bottom section of vineyard. The aspect is less steep, the soils are deeper, and this section receives longer sunlight in the afternoon. Thus, the M-G wine is richer, has more flesh, which arguably compliments the already generous, M-G style.

Never tasted the Roumier/Bonnefond wine. The Esmonin bottling is delicious, although less complex than Rousseau and M-G from the examples I have tasted. Never tasted the Pacalet bottling, or any other Ruchottes bottling, if memory serves.


Rousseau and Mugneret Gibourg both make a Ruchottes Chambertin. Question to those who are lucky enough to drink both: How do they compare in style and quality?

Rousseau and Mugneret Gibourg both make a Ruchottes Chambertin. Question to those who are lucky enough to drink both: How do they compare in style and quality?

Agreed, the Rousseau example is the most delicate and ethereal: generally quite red-fruited, with notes of peony and orange, without the new oak that the “big three” chez Rousseau see. It’s often one of Cyrielle’s favorites in the cellar, incidentally. Since the Mugneret parcel is right at the top of the Ruchottes du Bas, I don’t know that the geology is all that different - I think we’re still talking about very shallow soils over the bedrock. But it is definitely a bit fuller and deeper-pitched. The Roumier version is the most overtly structured out of the gates, and also aromatically deeper-pitched than the Rousseau bottling.

FWIW, in my limited experience with the Thomas Bassot version, it was quite a rich wine. But, by luck of the draw, most of my experience with that wine is with sunnier, riper vintages so that may inflect my perceptions.

But, on the other hand, there are so many Burgundy producers we don’t even see distributed in WA, and those we see are in small allocations with the upper level wines sometimes absent, and the pricing in general is higher through normal channels compared to internet pricing for the non “highly sought after” wines, as there is no great amount of competitive pricing…so whatever.

I think we are just arguing semantics…market vs. normal retail, and you can argue that what the market will bear is the “normal” or “new” retail. I am not getting my Rousseau at a discount over the normal let’s say 20-25% markup over wholesale that my retailer marks all his wines up. He is just not choosing to mark the Rousseau (nor the Dujac, etc.) up 500% over wholesale. And, as I said, the allocations are small…as in one bottle of the Chambertin nowadays, if that.

My point is not that this is the price everyone can buy it at. My point is more a criticism of the fact that retailers (and grey market suppliers) are taking advantage of the short supply and great demand to raise the prices to absurd levels, even though their wholesale prices may have stayed roughly the same, or gone up gradually. The distributor in our state that handles Wildman imports may be up to roughly $550-600 wholesale, but if the retailers (as in our state, getting it through regular distribution and not grey market) choose to sell it at $2000-2500, then that is their right to do so. But it should be recognized what they are choosing to do…as in 500% markups over their wholesale pricing. And if they choose to honor long term relationships with their customers and try to allocate wines in scarce supply to those who have been buying those wines over many years and supporting their businesses with other purchases, then that is also their decision to make.

The question of “to whom do the profits go?” is at the heart of this convo. I don’t see real argument about the market price, so if everyone in the value chain is willing to let the retail store accrue a large share of the economic value in the last leg that is fine, even though, really, it should get washed out over time by those up the value chain taking the profit for themselves. When stores can do this for idiosyncratic reasons like “I, vineyard owner, just believe prices should be x regardless of the market price” it gives them unusual power to play with that margin as they see fit - which is why the price people pay varies so wildly. It’s interesting that the intent of the vineyard owner is really to have that value accrue to the consumer but the vineyard owner doesn’t have the ability to ensure it.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I reread this. Interesting and informative.

Based on just the wines, I have no doubt Rousseau is one of the greatest expressions of Burgundy, and for most of my wine buying existence, it was quite affordable. In fact, I bought several cases of the Chambertin, Beze and CSJ 2002, but was forced to buy a case of one of the lesser Grand Crus at the same time. Happy days.

I loved what I drank, but as prices rose, I began to sell them. For me, the equation is the same: would I pay $1500 for a bottle. If no, I sell or trade it.

None of this alters the fact that I think Rousseau is the finest producer of Chambertin etc. Period. But I love the wines of Trapet and Rossignol Trapet. When I open a bottle of these, am I thinking about Rousseau’s Chambertin? God no. I am enjoying the wine in the glass. I have never tasted them side by side, but in isolation, no Rousseau has given me that much more pleasure than the Trapet 2005 that I opened last month.

Let’s relax and not worry that the wine is perhaps two points shy of the Rousseau. Comparisons are odious, and this one is a little moronic.
